meaning: Allowing an overall positive impression of a person to influence evaluations of their specific traits. Analogy: Assuming a physically attractive person is also kind, honest, and highly intelligent.
example: Our new manager is very polite and well-dressed, so I am sure he will write excellent software code.

meaning: The tendency to favor and trust members of one's own group over those who belong to other groups. Analogy: Giving a job promotion to a coworker just because you both support the same football team.
example: I voted for his proposal because he is in our department, even though the other department's plan was much better.

meaning: Perceiving members of an out-group as being highly similar to one another, while seeing in-group members as diverse. Analogy: Thinking all cats behave exactly the same way, while recognizing that your own three pet cats have completely unique personalities.
example: People from that other country all think the same way and have the exact same hobbies.

meaning: Underestimating situational influences and overestimating personal traits when explaining other people's behavior. Analogy: Assuming a driver who cuts you off in traffic is a selfish jerk, ignoring the possibility that they are rushing to the hospital.
example: She arrived late to the meeting, so she must be disorganized and lazy, rather than delayed by an accident.

meaning: Attributing personal successes to internal factors (like skill) while blaming personal failures on external factors (like bad luck). Analogy: Claiming you aced the exam because you are smart, but failed the next one because the teacher made it unfair.
example: Our team won because of my excellent strategy, but we lost the next game because the referee was biased.

meaning: The tendency to adopt behaviors, styles, or attitudes simply because others are doing so. Analogy: Buying a specific brand of shoes only because you see everyone at school wearing them.
example: She decided to support that political candidate only after seeing their high numbers in the latest polls.

meaning: The tendency to attribute our own actions to external, situational causes, while attributing others' behavior to internal, personal causes. Analogy: Explaining that you tripped because the rug was uneven, but assuming your friend tripped because they are clumsy.
example: I shouted because I was under extreme stress, but he shouted because he has an angry temperament.

meaning: The tendency to attribute greater accuracy to the opinion of an authority figure, regardless of content. Analogy: Saluting and obeying an order from someone just because they are wearing a uniform, even if the order makes no sense.
example: We must implement this project exactly as the director suggested, even though our data shows it will fail.

meaning: The tendency to defend and bolster the status quo, seeing existing social, economic, and political arrangements as fair and necessary. Analogy: A tenant defending high rent prices because 'that's just how the housing market is supposed to work.'
example: The current tax system might have some flaws, but changing it would create instability, so we should keep it exactly as it is.

meaning: Overestimating the degree to which other people share your beliefs, values, and habits. Analogy: Believing that because you love spicy food, the majority of people must also prefer spicy food.
example: Since everyone I know opposes the new policy, it is obvious that the entire city hates it.

meaning: The tendency for individuals with strong biases on an issue to perceive neutral media coverage as biased against their side. Analogy: Two opposing sports teams both claiming the referee was cheating in favor of the other side.
example: The news report on the election was completely biased against my favorite candidate, while supporters of the rival candidate complained about the exact same report.

meaning: Overestimating how easily others can read your internal mental and emotional states. Analogy: Giving a presentation and assuming the audience can hear your racing heartbeat and see your extreme nervousness, when you appear perfectly calm.
example: I was sure everyone in the meeting could tell how angry I was, but no one noticed anything unusual.

meaning: Believing that the world is inherently fair and that people get what they deserve (good things happen to good people, bad things to bad people). Analogy: Assuming a victim of a natural disaster must have done something bad to anger the universe.
example: He lost his job, so he must have been lazy or incompetent, rather than the victim of an economic downturn.

meaning: The belief that we see reality objectively and without bias, while those who disagree with us are uninformed, irrational, or biased. Analogy: Assuming your political opinions are pure logic, while anyone who votes differently has been brainwashed.
example: My views on the tax system are based on common sense, so anyone who disagrees must be corrupt or foolish.

meaning: The tendency of survey respondents to answer questions in a manner that will be viewed favorably by others. Analogy: Telling a pollster you exercise five times a week and eat healthy meals when you actually eat fast food on the couch.
example: Most survey participants reported that they recycle regularly, but the garbage analysis showed very little recycling actually occurred.

meaning: Overestimating the extent to which other people notice and observe your appearance, behavior, and mistakes. Analogy: Walking into a room with a tiny mustard stain on your shirt and believing everyone is staring at you in disgust.
example: I was mortified when I tripped slightly on the stairs, thinking the whole cafeteria was laughing at me, but no one had even looked up.

meaning: Believing that mass media messages have a greater effect on others than on oneself. Analogy: Thinking that advertising campaigns manipulate everyone else, while believing you only buy things out of pure necessity.
example: Violent video games make other teenagers aggressive, but they have absolutely no effect on my own behavior.

meaning: The tendency to evaluate out-group members more negatively and attribute bad motives to their actions. Analogy: Assuming a rival school's students are rude and dishonest, while believing your own school's students are friendly and honorable.
example: The opposing political party only proposed that bill to destroy our economy, not because they want to help the country.

meaning: The tendency for individuals to put forth less effort when working collectively in a group than when working individually. Analogy: Walking in a tug-of-war game and pulling less hard on the rope because there are ten other people pulling with you.
example: In the group science project, two members did almost all the work while the other three did nothing, knowing they would still get the same grade.

meaning: The phenomenon where individuals are less likely to offer help to a victim when other people are present. Analogy: Seeing someone drop their papers in a crowded hallway and walking past because you assume one of the other fifty people will help them.
example: When the car broke down in the middle of a busy highway, dozens of drivers passed by without calling for help, assuming someone else had already done so.

meaning: The practice of thinking or making decisions as a group in a way that discourages creativity or individual responsibility to avoid conflict. Analogy: A committee agreeing to launch a faulty rocket because no one wants to speak up and ruin the positive mood of the meeting.
example: The marketing team rushed to launch the campaign because no one wanted to disagree with the popular manager's idea.

meaning: Perceiving individuals as more attractive or appealing when they are in a group than when they are seen individually. Analogy: Looking at a group of flowers and thinking they look stunning, but realizing each individual flower is wilted and ordinary when examined closely.
example: In the band photograph, everyone looked incredibly cool, but when I met the bass player alone, he seemed completely ordinary.

meaning: A cognitive bias where a person who has performed a favor for someone is more likely to do another favor for them or like them more. Analogy: Liking a classmate more after lending them a pencil, because your brain convinces itself: 'I wouldn't lend them a pencil unless I liked them.'
example: I asked my rival to borrow a book, and after he lent it to me, he became much friendlier and cooperative.

meaning: The tendency to experience a decrease in empathy and willingness to help as the number of victims increases. Analogy: Feeling deeply moved by the story of one starving child, but feeling numb and disconnected when reading a report about a million people suffering from famine.
example: Donors contributed thousands of dollars to save a single stranded puppy, but ignored a charity appeal to save thousands of endangered birds.
